insight: TimeWave Security transforms stateless Wing evaluations into stateful ones: prior rejections accumulate as a pattern log, and once the threshold is crossed, future matching actions are blocked without requiring Augur rule authors to write any pattern-matching logic. The security benefit increases non-linearly with repeated attempts.

Semantic fingerprinting (category|file_extension) rather than content-based hashing is the correct primitive for grouping attack-class attempts. Two different API key rotation attempts map to the same pattern_hash because they share the vendor_secret_rotation category — which is what matters for detecting repeated intent, not repeated exact content.

Append-only log guarantee requires both API discipline (only mode=a file opens) and test verification (C.9 checks that query_events() adds zero lines and record_event() adds exactly 1). The append-only property is not enforced by OS-level immutability here — it's a behavioral contract of the module's public API. Tests verify the contract holds.

Injecting a synthetic AugurResult into the Augur results list before ConsensusLayer.arbitrate() is the cleanest integration point for TimeWave Security. The Consensus Layer treats it as a real critical Augur — no Consensus code modification needed. The injection point is the pre-arbitration AugurResult list, not the ConsensusDecision.

Bury mode and Sever mode address different cases: Bury is for material with accumulated forensic/historical value (Dragonrider Phase-Shift snapshots that show what harm was predicted); Sever is for clear-cut dead material with no forensic value. K517 ships Bury only. Sever is architecturally defined but K-future.

Catacombs (B121) was originally a dormant-Scribe repository. K517 extends it to also receive Angel-of-Death-Bury entries. One destination, two arrival modes: dormant-by-disuse (Scribes) vs sanctioned-relocation (Angel Bury). Both are forensic-recoverable; neither is active. The Catacombs substrate is more general than initially defined.

The rehydrate() function requires both operator (who) and rehydrate_reason (why) as required parameters. This design makes accidental rehydration unlikely and ensures every retrieval has a governance audit trail. The pattern: access to forensic evidence should itself be forensically recorded.

K514-K517 together form the complete Discipline Plane: Wing Enforce (K514) → Chronicler Remember (K515) → Bureau Observe (K515) → Dragonrider Evaluate-Hypothetically (K516) → TimeWave Security Learn-from-Rejection + Angel-of-Death Preserve-Forensics (K517). The security feedback loop is now closed: rejections inform future evaluations.

K517 C.10 shows 22.6ms p95 on 10k entries — 22× faster than the 500ms budget. The scan_limit=10,000 cap on match_security_pattern() provides bounded performance even on very large logs. For production logs that might exceed 10k entries, the cap ensures we evaluate the most recent entries (which are most relevant for pattern detection) within budget.

All K517 integration points in engine.py follow the same fail-safe pattern as K514-K516: try/except with pass/continue on failure. TimeWave Security pattern match failure → tw_match = None → no injection. Angel of Death bury failure → aod_burial_id = None → no bury record. The Wing's primary block decision is never compromised by security-layer failures.

timewave_security_enabled defaults to True (defense-in-depth, unlike dragonrider_enabled which defaults to False). TimeWave Security is lightweight (no sandbox fork, no deepcopy) and provides direct security value — appropriate to default-on. Dragonrider is expensive (sandbox fork + evaluation) and changes decisions on borderline cases — appropriate to default-off.

The Angel of Death's patent claim strength comes from multi-domain applicability: same severance agent class operates on Mimic-Trunk cleanup, Catacombs aging, TimeWave Security failed-quarantine, member-departure cleanup, deprecated-code pruning. K517 implements one domain (Dragonrider snapshot burial) — but the claim covers all domains. Bundling as standalone CJ #2305 (not under TimeWave Security #2302) preserves this breadth.
